summary: Fournier argues against adopting git-flow branching models simply because Git makes branching easy. She explains that git-flow was designed for large distributed open source teams with long development cycles, but most modern software teams sit together, release frequently, and benefit from continuous integration. Feature branching isolates developers, delays integration problems, and encourages all-or-nothing thinking about features when incremental, flagged releases work better for web deployments. She advocates for simpler workflows with just master and release branches rather than cargo-culting complex processes.
key_points: Git was designed for distributed open source teams with long release cycles, not co-located teams doing frequent releases
Feature branching isolates developers, delays code review and integration, and makes merge conflicts worse when everyone branches
Teams deploying to websites should learn to release code incrementally behind feature flags rather than thinking in all-or-nothing feature chunks
The git-flow develop branch and pristine master requirements add unnecessary complexity when simple tagging suffices
Just because a tool does something well doesn't mean that feature is important for your team's workflow
money_quotes: There are reasons beyond tool support that would lead one to want to encourage or discourage branching and merging, and mere tool support is not reason enough to embrace a branch-driven workflow.
A developer working on a branch is working alone.
Worst of all, it's easy for them to work in the dark, shielded from the eyes of other developers.
The burden of doing the right thing is entirely on the developer and good developers are lazy (or busy, or both).
From a workflow perspective, I want something that makes small problems come to light very early and obviously to the whole team, enabling inherent communication.
Feature branching also encourages thinking about code and features as all or none.
Learning how to write code in such a way as to be chunkable, flaggable, and almost always safe to go into production is a necessary skill set for frequent releases of any sort.
Just because a tool offers a feature, and does it well, does not mean that feature is actually important for your team.
key_insight: Teams should choose development workflows based on their actual needs—team structure, release frequency, and communication patterns—not because a popular tool makes a particular approach easy.
